title: 10 best herbal teas for gerd

remedies.remedy_desc: Chamomile tea helps with gerd because Chamomile tea helps with GERD, or gastroesophageal reflux disease, because it has anti-inflammatory and antispasmodic properties that can help reduce inflammation and relax the smooth muscles of the esophagus, thereby preventing acid reflux. Its calming effects may also help alleviate stress and anxiety, which can exacerbate GERD symptoms. Additionally, chamomile tea contains certain compounds, such as flavonoids and tannins, that have been shown to help reduce gastric acid production and promote proper digestion. Furthermore, chamomile tea has been used as a natural remedy for digestive issues for centuries, and some studies suggest that it may help improve symptoms of GERD. However, it is important to note that chamomile tea may interact with certain medications and should not be consumed in large quantities or by pregnant women without consulting a healthcare provider.
remedy_recipe: "Pour 1 cup of boiling water over 1-2 teaspoons of dried chamomile flowers, and let it steep for 5-10 minutes."
"Strain the tea to remove the chamomile flowers, and allow it to cool to a drinkable temperature."
"Optional: add a small amount of honey to taste for added flavor and potential soothing effects."
"Drink 1-2 cups of this tea throughout the day, as needed, but avoid drinking it right before bed as it may interfere with sleep."
"Consult with a healthcare provider if symptoms persist or worsen, as chamomile tea may interact with certain medications and is not recommended for everyone."

remedies.remedy_desc: Licorice tea helps with GERD because it contains substances called glycyrrhizic acids and flavonoids, which can help reduce inflammation and strengthen the mucosal lining of the esophagus. This can provide relief from symptoms such as heartburn, acid reflux, and stomach discomfort. Additionally, licorice tea has been shown to increase the mucus secretion in the gastrointestinal tract, which can protect the stomach lining from acid and help heal ulcers. Furthermore, licorice tea may help to increase the production of mucus in the stomach, which can act as a barrier against stomach acid, thereby reducing the symptoms of GERD. However, it is important to note that excessive consumption of licorice tea can lead to side effects such as high blood pressure and potassium loss, so it is recommended to consult with a healthcare provider before consuming licorice tea for GERD.
remedy_recipe: Gather 1 tablespoon of dried licorice root and place it in a tea infuser or teapot with a built-in strainer.
Boil 8 ounces of water and pour it over the licorice root, allowing it to steep for 10-15 minutes.
Optional: Add honey or lemon to taste for added flavor and potential soothing properties.
Let the tea cool down to a comfortable drinking temperature.
Enjoy the licorice tea, ideally sipping it slowly after meals to potentially help with GERD symptoms.

remedies.remedy_desc: Marshmallow tea helps with GERD because it contains a substance called mucilage, which creates a protective barrier in the digestive tract, reducing irritation and inflammation. This soothing effect can alleviate the symptoms of GERD, such as heartburn and acid reflux. Additionally, marshmallow tea has anti-inflammatory properties that can further help to reduce inflammation in the esophagus and stomach. Furthermore, it can increase the mucus production in the gut, which can provide additional protection to the digestive tract lining. Lastly, marshmallow tea is a natural remedy that is generally considered safe and has few side effects, making it a popular choice for those looking for a more holistic approach to managing GERD symptoms.
remedy_recipe: In a pot, combine 2 cups of water and 1 tablespoon of dried marshmallow root, simmer on low heat for 10-15 minutes (Marshmallow Tea for GERD, n.d.).
Strain the tea to remove marshmallow root solids, then let it cool down to a drinkable temperature (Marshmallow Tea for GERD, n.d.).
Stir in 1 teaspoon of honey or maple syrup, if desired, for added sweetness (Marshmallow Tea for GERD, n.d.).
Add a slice of lemon or a few drops of lemon juice for a refreshing taste and potential heartburn relief (Marshmallow Tea for GERD, n.d.).
Sip the marshmallow tea slowly, allowing it to coat and soothe the esophagus (Marshmallow Tea for GERD, n.d.).

remedies.remedy_desc: Slippery elm tea helps with GERD because it contains mucilage, a substance that becomes slippery and thick when mixed with water, providing a protective barrier to the digestive tract and relieving irritation. This soothing effect can alleviate the symptoms of gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD) by reducing inflammation and damage to the esophageal lining caused by stomach acid. Additionally, slippery elm tea is believed to increase the mucus secretion of the gastrointestinal tract, which can help to protect the stomach and intestines from harsh digestive juices. The demulcent properties of slippery elm tea can also help to heal any ulcers or wounds in the digestive tract, providing relief from GERD symptoms. Lastly, slippery elm tea is a natural remedy, making it a safer alternative to over-the-counter or prescription medications, which may have side effects or interact with other medications.
remedy_recipe: Ingredients: Slippery Elm Bark (preferably pre-cut and organic), 2 cups of water. Dosage: 1-2 tablespoons of slippery elm bark.
Preparation Time: Pour 2 cups of water into a pot and bring it to a boil.
Steeping Time: Add 1-2 tablespoons of slippery elm bark to the boiling water, then reduce heat and let it simmer for 2-3 minutes.
Straining Time: Remove the pot from heat, cover and let it steep for 5-10 minutes, then strain the tea to remove the bark.
Serving Time: Pour the tea into a cup, let it cool down to a drinkable temperature, and consume it up to three times a day as needed.

remedies.remedy_desc: Meadowsweet tea helps with GERD because it contains a natural substance called salicylic acid, which is a natural anti-inflammatory and can help reduce inflammation in the esophagus caused by stomach acid. Additionally, meadowsweet tea can help balance the stomach's pH levels, providing relief for GERD symptoms. It is also known to have antacid properties, which can neutralize stomach acid and prevent the symptoms of GERD. Furthermore, meadowsweet tea has been shown to stimulate the production of mucus in the stomach, forming a protective barrier against stomach acid. Lastly, meadowsweet tea is a natural alternative to traditional medications for GERD, with fewer side effects and a more gentle action on the stomach.
remedy_recipe: Crush 1 tablespoon of dried meadowsweet leaves and flowers, or 2 tablespoons of fresh ones, and steep in boiling water for 10 minutes (steep time can be adjusted to taste).
Strain the mixture to remove plant material.
Add honey or lemon to taste, if desired.
Let the tea cool down to a drinkable temperature.
Drink up to three cups a day, between meals, for GERD symptom relief.

remedies.remedy_desc: Catnip tea helps with GERD because it contains anti-inflammatory properties that can help reduce inflammation in the esophagus and stomach, which can alleviate GERD symptoms. Additionally, catnip has antispasmodic properties that can help relax the gastrointestinal muscles and reduce the frequency and intensity of acid reflux. Furthermore, catnip tea has been shown to improve digestion and reduce gas and bloating, which can help prevent the onset of GERD symptoms. Moreover, catnip contains calming compounds that can help reduce stress and anxiety, which can exacerbate GERD symptoms. Lastly, catnip tea is a natural and caffeine-free alternative to traditional medications for GERD, making it a safer and healthier option for long-term use.
remedy_recipe: Gather 1 teaspoon of dried catnip leaves and add to a tea infuser or teapot with a built-in strainer.
Boil 8 ounces of water and let it cool for 2-3 minutes before pouring it over the catnip in the teapot.
Allow the tea to steep for 5-10 minutes, depending on how strong you prefer the taste.
Remove the tea infuser or strain the tea into a cup to remove the catnip leaves.
Add honey or lemon to taste, if desired, and enjoy your homemade catnip tea for GERD.

remedies.remedy_desc: Ginger tea helps with gerd because Ginger tea helps with GERD, or gastroesophageal reflux disease, because it contains anti-inflammatory properties that can help reduce inflammation in the esophagus and stomach lining, alleviating some symptoms of GERD. Additionally, ginger is known to help relax the smooth muscle of the gastrointestinal tract, which can prevent the overproduction of stomach acid, a common cause of GERD. Furthermore, ginger has been shown to increase gastric emptying, which can help reduce the likelihood of acid backing up into the esophagus. Additionally, ginger contains natural antacids that can neutralize stomach acid and provide quick relief from GERD symptoms. Lastly, ginger tea can be consumed at a warm temperature, which can help stimulate the digestive system and improve overall digestion, further preventing GERD symptoms.
remedy_recipe: Peel and finely chop 1 tablespoon of fresh ginger root, let it steep in 1 cup of boiling water for 10 minutes (for ginger tea).
In a separate pot, boil 2 cups of water and add 1 teaspoon of dried chamomile flowers, let it simmer for 5 minutes (for chamomile tea).
Strain the chamomile tea into a teapot and add the ginger tea, mix in 1 tablespoon of honey and 1 tablespoon of lemon juice.
Combine 1/2 teaspoon of baking soda into the tea, stir until dissolved.
Serve hot, sip slowly and enjoy the soothing effects of this GERD-friendly ginger tea.

remedies.remedy_desc: Fennel tea helps with gerd because Fennel tea helps with GERD, or gastroesophageal reflux disease, because it contains volatile oils that relax the gastrointestinal tract, which can reduce symptoms of GERD such as bloating, gas, and cramping. These oils also have anti-inflammatory properties that can help soothe the inflammation in the esophagus caused by acid reflux. Additionally, fennel tea has been shown to increase the production of digestive enzymes, which can improve digestion and reduce the likelihood of acid reflux. Furthermore, fennel tea is a natural antispasmodic, which means it can help reduce spasms in the gastrointestinal tract and prevent acid reflux. Finally, fennel tea is a caffeine-free alternative to other beverages, which can help reduce acid production in the stomach and alleviate symptoms of GERD.
remedy_recipe: Gather 1 teaspoon of crushed fennel seeds and add them to a tea infuser or a piece of cheesecloth.
Place the fennel seeds in a cup and pour boiling water over them.
Allow the tea to steep for 5-10 minutes.
Remove the fennel seeds and optionally add honey or lemon to taste.
Drink the tea while warm, up to three times a day.

remedies.remedy_desc: Anise tea helps with gerd because Anise tea helps with GERD, or gastroesophageal reflux disease, because it has anti-inflammatory and antispasmodic properties that can help soothe the inflammation and relax the muscles in the digestive tract, reducing acid reflux and associated discomfort. Additionally, anise tea has been shown to stimulate the production of digestive enzymes, helping to break down food and reduce the likelihood of acid reflux. The licorice-like flavor of anise tea also helps to freshen breath and neutralize the taste of stomach acid, providing further relief for GERD symptoms. Furthermore, anise tea is caffeine-free, making it a gentler alternative to other teas and beverages that can exacerbate GERD symptoms. Finally, anise tea has been used traditionally as a natural remedy for digestive issues, including GERD, and its soothing and calming effects on the digestive system have been backed up by some scientific studies.
remedy_recipe: In a mortar, gently crush 1 teaspoon of anise seeds (for optimal benefits, use Pimpinella anisum species) for 2 minutes to release their aromatic oils.
Bring 2 cups of fresh water to a boil, then add the crushed anise seeds, reducing heat to a simmer for 5 minutes.
Remove the pot from heat, allowing the tea to steep for an additional 10 minutes to extract the beneficial compounds.
Strain the anise tea to remove the seeds, pouring the liquid into a cup.
Drink the anise tea while warm, up to three times a day after meals to help alleviate GERD symptoms.

remedies.remedy_desc: Skullcap tea helps with GERD because it has anti-inflammatory properties that can help reduce inflammation in the esophagus caused by stomach acid backing up into it. Additionally, skullcap tea contains antispasmodic properties that can help relax the muscles in the gastrointestinal tract, reducing the frequency and severity of GERD symptoms. Furthermore, skullcap tea has been shown to have anxiety-reducing effects, which can help alleviate stress-related GERD symptoms. It also helps to improve digestion and absorption of nutrients, reducing the amount of acid in the stomach and further preventing GERD symptoms. Lastly, skullcap tea is a natural alternative to conventional GERD medications, which can have side effects such as headaches, dizziness, and nausea, making it a safer and healthier option for long-term use.
remedy_recipe: Take 2 teaspoons of dried skullcap herb (Scutellaria lateriflora), rinse and add to a tea infuser.
Boil 1 cup of water, then pour over the infuser in a teapot or mug.
Let it steep for 10-15 minutes to allow the active compounds to be released.
Remove the infuser and discard the spent herb.
Drink the tea, up to three times a day, 30 minutes before meals, for best results in managing GERD symptoms.
Ingredients: Valerian root (2 teaspoons), water (4 cups), and lemon (1/2, squeezed). Optional: honey (1 teaspoon) for added flavor.
Preparation time: 15-20 minutes.
Pour water into a pot and bring it to a boil.
Add valerian root to boiling water, reduce heat, and let it simmer for 10-15 minutes.
Strain the tea, add lemon juice and honey (if desired), and let it cool down before drinking.
remedies.remedy_desc: Valerian tea helps with gerd because Valerian tea helps with GERD, or gastroesophageal reflux disease, because it contains natural compounds that can reduce inflammation and muscle spasms in the gastrointestinal tract. These compounds, including valerenic acid and hesperidin, have been shown to help relax the lower esophageal sphincter, a muscle that can contribute to GERD symptoms when it doesn't function properly. Additionally, valerian tea has antioxidant properties that may help protect the lining of the esophagus from damage caused by stomach acid. Furthermore, valerian tea can promote relaxation and reduce stress, which can indirectly help manage GERD by reducing symptoms brought on or worsened by anxiety or tension. However, it's important to note that while valerian tea may help alleviate some GERD symptoms, it should not be used as a substitute for medical treatment or advice from a healthcare professional.

remedy_recipe: Gather ingredients: 1 teaspoon of dried passionflower leaves (approximately 2 grams) and 1 cup of water.
Boil water and let it cool down for about 2 minutes until it is hot but not boiling.
Steep the passionflower leaves in the hot water for 5-10 minutes.
Strain the tea to remove the leaves and let it cool down to a drinkable temperature.
Enjoy the passionflower tea, it can be consumed up to three times a day for GERD relief.
remedies.remedy_desc: Passionflower tea helps with gerd because Passionflower tea helps with GERD, or gastroesophageal reflux disease, because it contains flavonoids, which have anti-inflammatory and antispasmodic properties. These properties can help reduce inflammation in the esophagus and alleviate the symptoms of GERD, such as heartburn, regurgitation, and stomach discomfort. Additionally, passionflower tea may help relax the smooth muscles in the gastrointestinal tract, which can prevent the backflow of stomach acid into the esophagus. Furthermore, passionflower tea has been shown to have anxiety-reducing effects, which can help reduce stress-related GERD symptoms. However, it's important to note that while passionflower tea may help alleviate GERD symptoms, it should not be used as a substitute for medical treatment or consultation with a healthcare provider.

remedy_recipe: Ingredients: Milk thistle seeds (2 teaspoons), fresh ginger (1 inch), water (4 cups), and honey (to taste).
Preparation time: 20 minutes.
Grind the milk thistle seeds and ginger to a coarse powder.
Bring water to a boil, add the powdered mixture, reduce heat, and simmer for 15 minutes.
Strain the tea, add honey to taste, and enjoy.
remedies.remedy_desc: Milk thistle tea helps with gerd because Milk thistle tea helps with GERD, or gastroesophageal reflux disease, because it contains a powerful antioxidant called silymarin. This antioxidant can help strengthen the mucous lining of the stomach, making it more resistant to acidic damage from stomach juices. Additionally, milk thistle tea has anti-inflammatory properties that can reduce inflammation in the esophagus and stomach, providing relief from GERD symptoms like heartburn and acid reflux. Furthermore, milk thistle tea has been shown to increase the production of bile, which aids in digestion and may help prevent the backflow of stomach acid into the esophagus. Finally, milk thistle tea is a natural remedy that lacks the side effects associated with prescription medications for GERD, making it a safer long-term option for managing this chronic condition.

remedy_recipe: Gather 2-3 handfuls of fresh dandelion flowers, ensuring they are from a pesticide-free area. Rinse the flowers gently to remove any dirt or debris, and let them dry. (Preparation time: 10-15 minutes)
In a pot, add 4 cups of water and bring it to a boil. Once boiling, add the dried dandelion flowers (approximately 2 cups) and reduce heat to low. Allow the mixture to simmer for 10-15 minutes.
Strain the tea through a fine-mesh sieve or cheesecloth into a teapot or heat-resistant container, discarding the spent flowers.
Optional: Add honey or lemon to taste, as these ingredients can help soothe the throat and balance the bitter taste of the dandelion tea. Stir well to ensure even distribution.
Serve the tea warm, dividing it into 3-4 cups. Enjoy this soothing beverage throughout the day, up to three times daily, to potentially alleviate GERD symptoms.
remedies.remedy_desc: Dandelion tea helps with GERD, or gastroesophageal reflux disease, because it is a natural diuretic and can help reduce inflammation in the stomach and digestive tract. The tea's anti-inflammatory properties come from its high concentration of antioxidants, which can help repair damaged cells in the esophagus and reduce the production of stomach acid. Dandelion tea also stimulates the production of bile, which aids in digestion and the breakdown of fats, reducing the strain on the stomach and lower esophageal sphincter. Additionally, dandelion tea has been shown to strengthen the muscles in the lower esophagus, helping to prevent the backward flow of stomach contents. However, it is important to note that while dandelion tea can be beneficial for some people with GERD, it may not be suitable for everyone, especially those taking certain medications or with underlying health conditions. As with any treatment, it is always best to consult with a healthcare provider before starting to consume dandelion tea for GERD.

Gather ingredients: 1 tablespoon of dried burdock root, 8 ounces of water, and honey (to taste, if desired).
Rinse burdock root and add to a pot, then pour in the water.
Bring the water to a boil, then reduce heat and let it simmer for 15-20 minutes.
Strain the tea to remove the burdock root and pour the liquid into a cup.
Add honey to taste and enjoy the soothing burdock tea, preferably 30 minutes before meals to aid in GERD symptoms.

Gather 2 cups of fresh stinging nettle leaves (select young, tender leaves for less bitter taste) and rinse them thoroughly; total preparation time, 15 minutes.
Crush or chop nettle leaves to release their natural oils, then place them in a heatproof pot or teapot.
Bring 4 cups of fresh water to a boil, then pour it over the crushed nettle leaves; let it steep for 5-10 minutes.
Strain the tea to remove the leaves, then let it cool down for a few minutes; recommended dosage, 3-4 cups a day.
For best results, consume nettle tea unsweetened or with a small amount of raw honey; enjoy within 24 hours for maximum benefits.
Gather ingredients: 1 teaspoon of dried alfalfa leaves (3g) and 1 cup of water (240ml).
Crush alfalfa leaves to release their oils, then put them in a tea infuser or a reusable tea bag.
Boil water and let it cool down for 2-3 minutes, so the temperature drops to about 85-90°C (185-195°F).
Place the tea infuser with alfalfa leaves in a cup and pour the hot water over it. Steep for 5-7 minutes.
Remove the tea infuser, let it cool down, and enjoy your Alfalfa tea. Sip it slowly, especially before meals, to help with GERD symptoms.
Gather ingredients: 1 teaspoon of dried rosehips and 8 ounces of water.
Rinse dried rosehips to ensure cleanliness, then add to pot.
Pour water into pot, bring to a boil, then let it simmer for 10-15 minutes.
Strain the tea to remove rosehips, leaving only the liquid.
Allow the tea to cool for a few minutes before drinking, and enjoy up to three cups daily for best results.

intro: Herbal teas, especially those made from chamomile, licorice root, marshmallow root, and slippery elm, can help alleviate Gastroesophageal Reflux Disease (GERD) symptoms. These herbs have anti-inflammatory properties that can reduce inflammation in the esophagus and stomach lining, providing relief from acid reflux and heartburn. Additionally, some herbs, like licorice root, can increase mucus production, which protects the esophageal lining from stomach acid. Furthermore, these teas can stimulate the production of digestive enzymes, helping to break down food more effectively and preventing the overproduction of stomach acid. The soothing effect of warm herbal teas can also help to relax the lower esophageal sphincter, preventing acid from backing up into the esophagus. Lastly, herbal teas are caffeine-free and can be a healthier alternative to traditional teas or coffee, which can exacerbate GERD symptoms.
